19: The parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus (Luke 16:19-31) is Luke's unique social-reversal parable with an afterlife scene. The rich man's clothing (purple and fine linen) are the markers of extreme wealth in antiquity (purple dye was extraordinarily expensive; fine linen was the priestly and royal cloth). Lazarus (the only named character in a Jesus parable) is a name meaning God has helped — the divine help he received was absent in life but present in death. The parable concludes with the theological point that Abraham's bosom (the Jewish term for the blessed state of the righteous after death) is not available to those who ignore the Law and the Prophets (Luke 16:29,31) — reinforcing Luke's consistent theme of wealth as a spiritual danger.
1: The Dishonest Manager parable (Luke 16:1–13) is unique to Luke and addressed to the disciples (not the Pharisees, who reappear in Luke 16:14). The parable’s setting is the Palestinian large-estate economy: a wealthy landowner employs an oikonomos (estate manager/steward) who manages the master’s debtors and accounts. The accusation of “wasting possessions” (diaskorpizōn) uses the same verb as the prodigal son’s waste (Luke 15:13), connecting the two parables.
2: “Give an account of your management” (apodos ton logon tēs oikonomias sou) — the rendering of accounts was a formal legal procedure; the steward is being dismissed and must account for all his transactions. The finality of “you cannot be manager any longer” means his window to act is the period between the accounting order and the final audit. The steward’s problem is both immediate (loss of employment) and future (no means of support after dismissal).
3: The steward’s self-assessment — “I’m not strong enough to dig, I’m ashamed to beg” — reveals class consciousness: manual labor (skaptō, digging) was associated with slaves and the poor; begging was social death for a person of any standing in the Mediterranean world. The steward’s predicament is that he has a white-collar skill set (accounts, relationships, negotiation) but is losing the position from which those skills were exercised.
4: “I know what I’ll do so that, when I lose my job here, people will welcome me into their homes” — the steward’s plan is to create social obligations among the master’s debtors by reducing their debts; when he loses his position, these debtors will owe him personal hospitality. The goal is not escape but relational capital — transforming economic transactions into social bonds. This is the Mediterranean patronage system’s logic applied deliberately and strategically.
5: The steward summons each debtor individually — the private one-on-one transaction ensures no debtor knows what others are being offered. The master’s debtors are agricultural tenants or merchants who owe the estate produce; their debts are substantial (100 bath-measures of oil, 100 kor of wheat). The steward still has access to the accounts and the legal authority to modify them before his dismissal is finalized; this is the window he exploits.
6: One hundred bath-measures of oil reduced to fifty — the batos was approximately 8 gallons; 100 bath was roughly 800 gallons of olive oil, a major agricultural commodity. The reduction of 50% is substantial. Some interpreters identify the removed portion as the steward’s own commission or interest rate that he was adding to the master’s bills — making the reduction not fraud against the master but self-sacrifice of the steward’s own profit margin. This interpretation makes the master’s subsequent commendation less scandalous.
7: One hundred kor of wheat reduced to eighty — the koros was approximately 10-12 bushels; 100 kor was a very large wheat debt. The 20% reduction is smaller in percentage than the oil reduction (50%) but larger in absolute terms. If the removed portions represent the steward’s own surcharges, he is buying future hospitality by sacrificing his own income. The parallel structure of the two transactions (bill alteration, quick document-writing) emphasizes the steward’s shrewd, decisive action under time pressure.
8: “The master commended the dishonest manager because he had acted shrewdly” — the master’s commendation is the most difficult element of the parable. The commendation is for phrōnimōs (shrewdly, practically-wisely) acting, not for honesty. “The people of this world are more shrewd in dealing with their own kind than are the people of the light” — the disciples are being challenged to deploy equal prudence and strategic thinking for the kingdom that the world’s people deploy for their economic interests.
9: “Use worldly wealth (mamōna tēs adikias) to gain friends for yourselves” — mamōnas is the Aramaic word for wealth/property (not pejorative in itself); “of unrighteousness” qualifies it as belonging to the fallen age. The instruction is to use money the way the steward used it: to create relationships and obligations that will outlast the money. “When it is gone, you will be welcomed into eternal dwellings” (aionious skēnās) — the eternal equivalent of the steward’s “welcome me into their homes” (Luke 16:4).
10: “Whoever can be trusted with very little can also be trusted with much” — the faithfulness-scales principle moves from the steward’s situation to a general principle about character: trustworthiness is a consistent character quality that shows itself in small and large contexts alike. The Mishnah (Avot Luke 4:2: “one mitzvah leads to another mitzvah”) applies the same principle to obedience; faithfulness in small things is the training ground for trustworthiness in large things.
11: “If you have not been trustworthy in handling worldly wealth, who will trust you with true riches?” — the contrast between mamōnas (worldly wealth) and “true riches” (alēthinon) frames money as a test-case for handling the real thing. In the Jewish wisdom tradition, wisdom and the fear of the Lord are the true riches (Proverbs 3:14; Luke 8:10–11; Sir Luke 4:1–10). Faithfulness in the economy of this age is the prerequisite for participation in the economy of the age to come.
12: “Trustworthy with someone else’s property” — worldly wealth is framed as allotrion (another’s property, belonging to someone else), not truly yours. The steward’s situation illustrates this: he managed wealth that was not his; his failure was that he wasted it as if it were his own. The disciple who manages worldly wealth faithfully demonstrates the character needed for “property of your own” (hymeteran, your own) — the eschatological inheritance of the kingdom.
13: “No one can serve two masters” — the slave-owner social structure makes the metaphor concrete: a slave owned by two masters would have divided loyalties in every specific task; the masters’ competing claims would make every decision a conflict. Mamōnas as a quasi-divine power competing with God for ultimate allegiance is the Lukan wealth theology underlying Luke 12, 16, 18–19; the conclusion is that the disciples must choose their master — not manage a compromise between two absolute claims.
14: The Pharisees’ love of money (philargyroi, money-loving) is Luke’s characterization that connects the teaching of Luke 16:1–13 to the following controversy. Their sneering (exemuktērizon, turned up their noses, lit. mocked with the nose) echoes Psalms 22:7 (the sufferer mocked by those who stick out their lips and shake their heads). Josephus does not particularly associate Pharisees with wealth; Luke’s characterization may reflect the critique that the Pharisees’ purity and tithing system benefited the wealthy who could afford to maintain it.
15: “You are the ones who justify yourselves in the eyes of others, but God knows your hearts” — the contrast between outward self-justification (dikaiountes heautous enōpion tōn anthrōpōn) and divine heart-knowledge. “God knows your hearts” (kardiognōstēs) is an explicit divine attribute (Acts 1:24; 15:8 apply it to God and Jesus). “What people value highly is detestable in God’s sight” (bdelygma enōpion tou theou) — the OT language of idolatry applied to the wealth-prestige system: the love of money is a form of idolatry.
16: “The Law and the Prophets were proclaimed until John. Since that time, the good news of the kingdom of God is being proclaimed” — Luke’s clearest statement of the salvation-historical turning point; Acts 13:24–25 and 19:4 use John as the hinge of the two eras. “Everyone is forcing their way into it” (pas eis autēn biazetai) — the kingdom is being entered with urgency and force; the passive may be “everyone is being urged into it” or the active “everyone is pressing into it violently.” Luke’s context suggests the urgency of the kingdom proclamation: the open door is being pressed through.
17: “It is easier for heaven and earth to disappear than for the least stroke of a pen to drop out of the Law” — the permanent validity of Torah is asserted immediately after the announcement of a new salvation-historical era. The “least stroke of a pen” (the keraia, the smallest mark in a Hebrew letter, equivalent to a serif) echoes Matthew 5:18’s “not one jot or tittle.” Luke’s placement between Luke 16:16 (kingdom-era) and Luke 16:18 (divorce) suggests that the kingdom does not nullify Torah but fulfills and intensifies it.
18: The divorce saying — brief and without exception clause (unlike Matthew 5:32’s “except for marital unfaithfulness”) — is placed between the Torah-permanence saying (Luke 16:17) and the Rich Man and Lazarus parable (Luke 16:19–31). The context is the Law’s application in the kingdom age: Jesus’s one-flesh teaching (Genesis 2:24) makes divorce-and-remarriage equivalent to adultery. The Pharisaic school of Hillel permitted divorce on relatively broad grounds (Deuteronomy 24:1, “something objectionable”); the school of Shammai permitted it only for sexual immorality; Jesus’s ruling is stricter than either.
20: Lazarus at the gate (pylōn, the formal entrance to a substantial house) — the only named character in a Jesus parable; the name “Lazarus” (El’azar, God has helped) is a theological irony: the one whose very name declares divine help receives none from the rich man at whose gate he lies. The gate (pylon) in an ancient Mediterranean city was the public face of the household’s wealth and status; Lazarus’s placement there makes the rich man’s failure to help inescapably deliberate.
21: “Even the dogs came and licked his sores” — dogs (kynaria) in antiquity were not domestic pets but scavengers; they gathered wherever there was food, refuse, or disease. Their licking of Lazarus’s sores is not comfort but further degradation — the contact of unclean animals with his wounds. The table-scraps (psichiōn, crumbs) that fall from the rich man’s table are the minimum of social charity; the contrast with the “fine food” of the rich man’s daily feasting makes the neglect inexcusable. “No one gave him anything” echoes Luke 15:16 (the prodigal’s situation).
22: “Angels carried him to Abraham’s side” (eis ton kolpon Abraam) — Abraham’s bosom/lap was the Jewish term for the blessed state after death; Josephus describes the righteous as resting “in the bosom of Abraham” (War 2.155). The angelic escort of the righteous soul to Paradise is found in T. Asher Luke 6:4–6 and other Second Temple sources. The rich man’s burial (etaphe) is mentioned in contrast: an elaborately prepared earthly burial vs. an angelic escort to heavenly rest — the final inversion of their earthly positions.
23: Hades — the Greek term for the underworld (translating Hebrew Sheol); in Second Temple Jewish thought, Hades contained both the righteous and wicked in separate sections (4 Ezra 7:85–87; 1 En. Luke 22:9–14; Luke 23:43 uses “paradise” for the blessed section). The rich man can see Lazarus in the distance — the reversal is visible: the beggar who lay at the gate, unseen in his need, is now seen in Abraham’s embrace; the rich man who feasted in visible luxury now suffers in visible torment.
24: “Father Abraham, have pity on me” — the rich man’s appeal to Abraham as “father” invokes Jewish ethnic identity: John the Baptist had warned that claiming Abraham as father was insufficient (Luke 3:8). The request for Lazarus to bring water (a single drop on the tongue) reveals the rich man’s continued assumption that Lazarus is available for errand-running — the persistence of the patron-client power structure that characterized his earthly life. He still thinks of Lazarus as below him, a person to be sent.
25: Abraham’s reply “Son, remember” (teknon, mnēsthēti) — the address as “son” (teknon) confirms the genetic-covenantal relationship while explaining the reversal. The principle is the Lukan Beatitudes and Woes in concentrated form: you received your good things, Lazarus received bad things; now the positions are reversed. The finality of the eschatological reversal is the counterpart to the Magnificat’s vision (Luke 1:52–53): what God will do, he has now permanently done.
26: “A great chasm has been set in place” (chasma mega estēriktai) — the verb stērizō (to fix firmly, establish permanently) — the same word used for Jesus “setting his face” toward Jerusalem (Luke 9:51) and for the Tower of Siloam (Luke 13:4). The permanence of the post-mortem state is the parable’s structural claim: the reversals of the eschaton are fixed and irreversible. This is not systematic afterlife theology but a warning embedded in a parable: the eschatological reversal is real and final.
27: The second request — “send Lazarus to my family” — shifts from personal relief to prophetic warning for the five brothers still alive. Having failed to get relief, the rich man asks for a resurrection-appearance to warn his kin. The motivation may be genuine concern for his brothers — possibly the first moment of care for others he has expressed — or it may be a continuing use of Lazarus as an instrument. Either way, the request reveals the rich man’s continued assumption that miraculous intervention is what converts people.
28: “Warn them, so that they will not also come to this place of torment” — the five brothers represent those still alive who have the same wealth and same blindness as their dead brother. The protective motivation mirrors the OT prophetic concern for the household (Ezekiel 3:17–19; Luke 33:7–9: the watchman must warn). But the mechanism proposed (a resurrection-appearance) is precisely what the rich man thinks will accomplish what Torah and the Prophets have failed to do.
29: “They have Moses and the Prophets; let them listen to them” — Abraham’s answer asserts the radical sufficiency of scripture. The Torah and the Prophets contain everything needed to produce the repentance and generosity that would have prevented the rich man’s situation. Deuteronomy 15:7–11 (open your hand to the poor), Isaiah 58:6–7 (share your food with the hungry), Ezekiel 16:49 (the sin of Sodom: failure to help the poor and needy) — the scripture is not silent about economic obligation to Lazarus-figures.
30: “If someone from the dead goes to them, they will repent” — the rich man’s assumption is that a miraculous sign — a resurrection-appearance — would accomplish what scripture has not. This assumption lies behind many requests for signs throughout Luke (Luke 11:16,29; Luke 23:8). The assumption is also the Sadducean position in reverse: those who deny resurrection think a resurrection-appearance would be overwhelming proof; the parable’s answer challenges both the assumption that extraordinary signs convert and the premise that scripture is insufficient.
31: “If they do not listen to Moses and the Prophets, they will not be convinced even if someone rises from the dead” — the conclusion is a direct Lukan pointer to the resurrection of Jesus: those who rejected the OT witness (the leaders of Israel who knew Moses and the Prophets) were not convinced by the resurrection of Jesus (Acts 2–5 documents the rejection). The parable’s final word frames the entire Gospel’s narrative arc: the resurrection will happen, and some will not believe it because they have already refused to hear what Moses and the Prophets said about it.
